ZKPs essentially let someone prove that they know or have something without=
 giving up any information about what they know or have.
For example, I could prove that I know the password to an account without e=
ntering the password and risking its exposure, or that I have enough money =
to cover rent for the next year without telling some random broker all of t=
he details of my personal finances. The technology has implications for per=
sonal privacy, crypto, businesses, and even nuclear disarmament.=C2=A0
Because of all of the potential use cases, and because ZKPs are so hard to =
grasp, they are ripe for the Hype Cycle. As Jill tweeted:=C2=A0

What=E2=80=99s ultimately so promising about ZKPs is that they have the pot=
ential to eliminate a major trade-off inherent in living, working, and tran=
sacting online: the convenience, speed, reach, and scale of the internet in=
 exchange for our privacy. They also allow for nuance in a privacy landscap=
e that=E2=80=99s often black-and-white.=C2=A0
When ZKPs are at-scale, people and companies may no longer have to assume t=
hat privacy is unattainable or overly constrictive, which both has predicta=
ble direct benefits and opens up a new design space for unpredictable innov=
ation.=C2=A0

What Are Zero-Knowledge Proofs?
Our data is everywhere. Names, dates of birth, email addresses, credit card=
 numbers, the addresses we have lived at in the last five years, our mother=
s=E2=80=99 maiden names=E2=80=A6 These are just a few of the near infinite =
bits of information on ourselves that we all fork over every day to compani=
es, social media sites, customer service representatives, and sometimes (un=
wittingly) to scammers.=C2=A0
The issues with this state of affairs are self-evident. Identity theft, ema=
il compromise, data breaches, and other forms of fraud cost individuals and=
 businesses tens of billions of dollars per year and orders of magnitude mo=
re than that in spending on defense and prevention. This is not to mention =
the headaches of dealing with the fallout.
The proliferation of data and its associated vulnerability has come to be a=
n accepted cost of doing business as a participant in the modern, interconn=
ected world. We have to trust each other. We have to enter our credit card =
numbers into websites. We have to send our landlord our credit history. We =
have to give our bank our social security numbers. And it=E2=80=99s not jus=
t us as individuals: companies and institutions have to disclose sensitive =
information to each other all the time in order to run their businesses. Sh=
aring information and accepting its insecurity is a necessary sacrifice in =
order for society as we know it to function.
But what if there was a way to conduct these interactions and transactions =
with the same levels of trust and certainty without sharing all of this dat=
a? Well, thanks to a tricky little bit of mathematics that even cryptograph=
ers refer to as =E2=80=9Cmoon math,=E2=80=9D this is possible. Enter: the z=
ero-knowledge proof.
Using this technique, I can prove to you that I know something without disc=
losing to you the information that I know. Without getting into the moon ma=
th itself, it works something like the following.
My color blind friend and I are looking at balls on a table that are identi=
cal except that one is red and the other green. He is not sure that he beli=
eves me when I tell him they are two different colors. We decide to establi=
sh that they are, in fact, different colors by playing a game:=C2=A0
I give him the two balls to hide behind his back.=C2=A0
He takes one ball out and shows it to me.=C2=A0
Then he puts this ball back behind his back, withdraws his hand again, show=
s me a ball and asks, =E2=80=9CDid I switch the balls?=E2=80=9D=C2=A0
If we repeat this game enough times, and if I answer correctly every time, =
then I will demonstrate to him that the balls are almost certainly two diff=
erent colors. Importantly, here, I have proven this to him without revealin=
g any other information. Perhaps frustratingly for him, he still doesn=E2=
=80=99t know which ball is red and which ball is green.

The History of Zero-Knowledge
Zero-knowledge proofs are not new: they were first designed and devised in =
the 1980=E2=80=99s by MIT researchers Shafi Goldwasser, Silvio Micali, and =
Charles Rackoff.=C2=A0
The trio were working on a problem related to a concept called an interacti=
ve proof system. In such a system, there are two parties: a prover of some =
information and a verifier of that information. Generally in these systems,=
 it is assumed that the prover cannot be trusted while the verifier can be.=
 It is the goal of the system to be designed in such a way that:=C2=A0
The verifier can be convinced of a true statement by an untrusted prover, a=
nd=C2=A0
That it is impossible for the prover to convince the verifier of an untrue =
statement.
Think of it this way: I am in line outside of a bar trying to get in. There=
 is a bouncer standing at the door who asks to check my ID. In this scenari=
o, I am the prover (of my age) and the bouncer is the verifier. The bouncer=
 does not know if he can trust me. If the bouncer and I use an interactive =
proof system, and I am over 21, I will be able to demonstrate to him that I=
 am over 21. If the guy behind me is underage, he will not be able to convi=
nce the bouncer to let him in.
Goldwasser, Micali, and Rackoff added an additional layer of complexity to =
the whole interaction. They asked (and answered) the question: how do we ha=
ndle it if, not only can the bouncer not trust me -- but I also cannot trus=
t the bouncer. Maybe I suspect that the bouncer has been stealing people=E2=
=80=99s identities. Maybe I don=E2=80=99t want him to know that I=E2=80=99m=
 a Taurus. Whatever the issue is, suddenly the prover does not want the ver=
ifier to know the underlying information.
Using what those MIT academics introduced as a zero-knowledge proof, I can =
convince the bouncer to let me in without ever telling him my actual birthd=
ate.
Over the course of the 1980=E2=80=99s and 1990=E2=80=99s, zero-knowledge pr=
oofs enjoyed further research in academia but very little in the way of act=
ual implementation or use. It was not until the new millennium that researc=
hers and entrepreneurs started applying the theory to practical application=
s.
One of the first such applications, in the mid-2000=E2=80=99s, was the use =
of zero-knowledge proofs in password security.=C2=A0
When you go to login to a website using a password, often what happens is t=
hat you type in your password and send it to the server, which condenses yo=
ur password into a string of gobbledygook and then compares it to see if it=
 matches the gobbledygook they have stored next to your name in their syste=
m. If the values are the same, you=E2=80=99re in. The gobbledygook is there=
 as a layer of password protection: your password cannot be derived from it=
 and it keeps the company or whoever from storing your password as plaintex=
t. However, there is still an issue here: you are still disclosing your pas=
sword to the server in the first place! Protocols for using zero-knowledge =
proofs to solve this vulnerability were uncovered over the course of the 20=

w7tg_NNZl6gQB2Y4-zaCyJXWnJlDOWfvwcorqVUQhg0nOM-VOXf9A-Jh4oM]: some of the f=
irst examples of applying ZKPs to a real-world problem.
Lots more was written and explored over that decade around how zero-knowled=
ge systems could be applied to identity and authentication problems. But th=
e real inflection point for zero-knowledge happened years later.
In 2013 and 2014, zero-knowledge proofs found commercial application in ano=
ther system that spent many years incubating in academia before being intro=
duced to the world: cryptocurrency. Despite the fact that cryptocurrencies =
tend to have a reputation for being good for use in illicit transactions, a=
 core feature of bitcoin and most other cryptocurrencies is that everything=
 that happens with them is publicly recorded. All you need to look at are c=
ases like the Silk Road bust [https://email.mg2.substack.com/c/eJxFUc3K5CAQ=
fJrxZvA3MQcPHyzfawSNnUQm0UE7G_L268wcFqSK7qaptmp2CGsut33liuQNE94vsAmuugMiFHJ=

tFDlr3ShnSlENuW-m_6D8cIa_P] or, more recently, the Colonial Pipeline ransom=
ware crackdown [https://email.mg2.substack.com/c/eJxFkU1uxCAMhU8z7BKFf7Jg0U=
2vEUFwEloCEZBGuX2ZmUqVLIxl-T38MZsKa8q3PlKp6HlM9T5AR7hKgFoho7NAnrzTWBBFORuR0=

is in fact completely transparent.
Cue the introduction of Zcash (originally called Zerocash [https://email.mg=
2.substack.com/c/eJxNkM1uhDAMhJ9mc1uUXxIOOfTS10AhMZAuJCiYtvTpm10OrWTZkmdGI3=

9BglLZh97hk6xotRKtMUxcMCo9oZVqpTSkNodcU-mv9Bc-CJIZ], as well as its predece=
ssor Zerocoin [https://email.mg2.substack.com/c/eJxFkE2OwyAMhU9TlhG_ARYsZtN=
rRATcFDWBCJyZye2HNouRLNvy09OTv-ARllJPt5eG5N0mPHdwGX7aCohQydGgTik6NnIjlLQkOh=

FeCHAi-QHyrRAhtoJx8njm58YtRKjMUxcL3dGQquOQhrSk2Pprvwf-gcxlYju]). Zcash [htt=
ps://email.mg2.substack.com/c/eJxFkEuOhDAMRE_TWSLyMQmLLGbT10D5uCFqSFASZsScf=
kKzGMmyLZespypnKs4pn3pPpZKrTfXcUUf8KSvWipkcBfMUvKYDUxzESLwWnipQJJTplRE3E1Zd=
84FkP-wanKkhxc9H3zMgi5ZOit4yb2EU10lYGIC9RuRAueLDzTWHDxgdavzGfKaIZNVLrXt58K8=

FVm3KIc5Pfi9lw_QjNyNTmdsRQzwmjsSv622O9o_q4nmaMmFuEfjL1CogPEvigFOW3pxYClwCDE=
Io0sk_tK_5D_wDDBn1s], introduced in 2014 and launched in 2016, uses a speci=
fic type of zero-knowledge system to create a cryptocurrency that maintains=
 the decentralized properties of something like bitcoin while introducing p=
rivacy-preserving properties much closer to those of physical cash -- which=
 is to say near total lack of traceability.=C2=A0
In Zcash, zero-knowledge proofs specifically enable the network of computer=
s running the Zcash protocol to verify that every transaction is valid (i.e=
=2E I actually have the 10 Zcash I am se=
nding to you) while maintaining the p=
rivacy of the transaction data.
If you want to learn more, you should give this 2017 Radiolab episode on Zc=
ash a listen:
Zcash and other privacy-oriented cryptocurrencies have attracted a great de=
al of attention -- and money. The market cap of Zcash recently approached $=
4 billion (before retracing=E2=80=A6 this stuff is volatile). All of this m=
oney and attention has led to a dramatic acceleration of breakthroughs in z=
ero-knowledge systems, particularly when it comes to implementing them.
A new generation of efficient zero-knowledge proofs have been developed tha=
t solve many of the issues that previously surrounded the clunky, burdensom=
e, expensive, and controversial setups of these systems. (If you listened t=
o that RadioLab episode, many of the problems discussed in it are no longer=
 problems=E2=80=A6 only 5 years later!)=C2=A0
Optimizations have led to huge advancements in the speed and scalability of=
 these systems. A new and larger community of builders have cropped up lead=
ing to implementation improvements. Active open source development of libra=
ries around these systems are accelerating development and increasing perfo=
rmance.
The moment for zero-knowledge systems to take off is finally upon us.
To learn more about the upside of hype, the privacy spectrum, how ZKPs will=
 actually be used, both in crypto and beyond, and the ZKP design space=E2=

em 0;">What’s ultimately so promising about ZKPs is that they have th=

a1a; font-size: 16px; line-height: 26px; margin: 0 0 1em 0;">When ZKPs are =

efits and opens up a new design space for unpredictable innovation. </=
p><p style=3D"color: #1a1a1a; font-size: 16px; line-height: 26px; margin: 0=

<p style=3D"color: #1a1a1a; font-size: 16px; line-height: 26px; margin: 0 0=
 1em 0;">Our data is everywhere. Names, dates of birth, email addresses, cr=
edit card numbers, the addresses we have lived at in the last five years, o=
ur mothers’ maiden names… These are just a few of the near infi=
nite bits of information on ourselves that we all fork over every day to co=
mpanies, social media sites, customer service representatives, and sometime=
s (unwittingly) to scammers. </p><p style=3D"color: #1a1a1a; font-size=
: 16px; line-height: 26px; margin: 0 0 1em 0;">The issues with this state o=
f affairs are self-evident. Identity theft, email compromise, data breaches=
, and other forms of fraud cost individuals and businesses tens of billions=
 of dollars per year and orders of magnitude more than that in spending on =
defense and prevention. This is not to mention the headaches of dealing wit=
h the fallout.</p><p style=3D"color: #1a1a1a; font-size: 16px; line-height:=
 26px; margin: 0 0 1em 0;">The proliferation of data and its associated vul=
nerability has come to be an accepted cost of doing business as a participa=
nt in the modern, interconnected world. We have to trust each other. We hav=
e to enter our credit card numbers into websites. We have to send our landl=
ord our credit history. We have to give our bank our social security number=
s. And it’s not just us as individuals: companies and institutions ha=
ve to disclose sensitive information to each other all the time in order to=
 run their businesses. Sharing information and accepting its insecurity is =
a necessary sacrifice in order for society as we know it to function.</p><p=
 style=3D"color: #1a1a1a; font-size: 16px; line-height: 26px; margin: 0 0 1=
em 0;">But what if there was a way to conduct these interactions and transa=
ctions with the same levels of trust and certainty without sharing all of t=
his data? Well, thanks to a tricky little bit of mathematics that even cryp=
tographers refer to as “moon math,” this is possible. Enter: <s=
trong>the zero-knowledge proof</strong>.</p><p style=3D"color: #1a1a1a; fon=
t-size: 16px; line-height: 26px; margin: 0 0 1em 0;">Using this technique, =
I can prove to you that I know something without disclosing to you the info=
rmation that I know. Without getting into the moon math itself, it works so=
mething like the following.</p><p style=3D"color: #1a1a1a; font-size: 16px;=
 line-height: 26px; margin: 0 0 1em 0;">My color blind friend and I are loo=
king at balls on a table that are identical except that one is red and the =
other green. He is not sure that he believes me when I tell him they are tw=
o different colors. We decide to establish that they are, in fact, differen=
t colors by playing a game: </p><ul style=3D"margin: 1em 0;"><li style=
=3D"margin: 3.75px 0 3.75px 7.5px; mso-special-format: bullet;"><p style=3D=
"color: #1a1a1a; font-size: 16px; line-height: 26px; margin: 0;">I give him=
 the two balls to hide behind his back. </p></li><li style=3D"margin: =
3.75px 0 3.75px 7.5px; mso-special-format: bullet;"><p style=3D"color: #1a1=
a1a; font-size: 16px; line-height: 26px; margin: 0;">He takes one ball out =
and shows it to me. </p></li><li style=3D"margin: 3.75px 0 3.75px 7.5p=
x; mso-special-format: bullet;"><p style=3D"color: #1a1a1a; font-size: 16px=
; line-height: 26px; margin: 0;">Then he puts this ball back behind his bac=
k, withdraws his hand again, shows me a ball and asks, “Did I switch =
the balls?” </p></li></ul><div class=3D"captioned-image-containe=

x; line-height: 26px; margin: 0 0 1em 0;">The trio were working on a proble=
m related to a concept called an <strong>interactive proof system</strong>.=
 In such a system, there are two parties: a <strong>prover</strong> of some=
 information and a <strong>verifier</strong> of that information. Generally=
 in these systems, it is assumed that the prover cannot be trusted while th=
e verifier can be. It is the goal of the system to be designed in such a wa=
y that: </p><ul style=3D"margin: 1em 0;"><li style=3D"margin: 3.75px 0=
 3.75px 7.5px; mso-special-format: bullet;"><p style=3D"color: #1a1a1a; fon=
t-size: 16px; line-height: 26px; margin: 0;">The verifier can be convinced =
of a true statement by an untrusted prover, and </p></li><li style=3D"=
margin: 3.75px 0 3.75px 7.5px; mso-special-format: bullet;"><p style=3D"col=
or: #1a1a1a; font-size: 16px; line-height: 26px; margin: 0;">That it is imp=
ossible for the prover to convince the verifier of an untrue statement.</p>=
</li></ul><p style=3D"color: #1a1a1a; font-size: 16px; line-height: 26px; m=
argin: 0 0 1em 0;">Think of it this way: I am in line outside of a bar tryi=
ng to get in. There is a bouncer standing at the door who asks to check my =
ID. In this scenario, I am the prover (of my age) and the bouncer is the ve=
rifier. The bouncer does not know if he can trust me. If the bouncer and I =
use an interactive proof system, and I am over 21, I will be able to demons=
trate to him that I am over 21. If the guy behind me is underage, he will n=
ot be able to convince the bouncer to let him in.</p><p style=3D"color: #1a=
1a1a; font-size: 16px; line-height: 26px; margin: 0 0 1em 0;">Goldwasser, M=
icali, and Rackoff added an additional layer of complexity to the whole int=
eraction. They asked (and answered) the question: <strong>how do we handle =
it if, not only can the bouncer not trust me -- but I also cannot trust the=
 bouncer</strong>. Maybe I suspect that the bouncer has been stealing peopl=
e’s identities. Maybe I don’t want him to know that I’m a=
 Taurus. Whatever the issue is, suddenly the prover does not want the verif=
ier to know the underlying information.</p><p style=3D"color: #1a1a1a; font=
-size: 16px; line-height: 26px; margin: 0 0 1em 0;">Using what those MIT ac=
ademics introduced as a zero-knowledge proof, I can convince the bouncer to=
 let me in without ever telling him my actual birthdate.</p><p style=3D"col=
or: #1a1a1a; font-size: 16px; line-height: 26px; margin: 0 0 1em 0;">Over t=
he course of the 1980’s and 1990’s, zero-knowledge proofs enjoy=
ed further research in academia but very little in the way of actual implem=
entation or use. It was not until the new millennium that researchers and e=
ntrepreneurs started applying the theory to practical applications.</p><p s=
tyle=3D"color: #1a1a1a; font-size: 16px; line-height: 26px; margin: 0 0 1em=
 0;">One of the first such applications, in the mid-2000’s, was the u=
se of zero-knowledge proofs in password security. </p><p style=3D"colo=
r: #1a1a1a; font-size: 16px; line-height: 26px; margin: 0 0 1em 0;">When yo=
u go to login to a website using a password, often what happens is that you=
 type in your password and send it to the server, which condenses your pass=
word into a string of gobbledygook and then compares it to see if it matche=
s the gobbledygook they have stored next to your name in their system. If t=
he values are the same, you’re in. The gobbledygook is there as a lay=
er of password protection: your password cannot be derived from it and it k=
eeps the company or whoever from storing your password as plaintext. Howeve=
r, there is still an issue here: you are still disclosing your password to =
the server in the first place! Protocols for using zero-knowledge proofs to=
 solve this vulnerability <a href=3D"https://email.mg2.substack.com/c/eJxFk=
MuOwyAMRb-m7AbxhixYzGZ-I-JhUtSERASmyt8PbRcjWbZl--rKJ7gGy14ve-xnQ680t-sAW-B5=
rtAaVNRPqHOOlipmuBQTilZEaqRB-ZxTBdhcXm2rHdDR_ZqDa3kvbwUhTKK7DUomDk6xBFwkT7R=
jwHTiZvKEqyl9fF2PGUoAC79Qr70AWu29teO88e8b-xnxBH-4BU7cSwgYYh-zcfh0ZRkd5Ypj9s=
UIMfiICWXLCKNEUUGZlHLCFLMQJx0ZyOQjAWMmQag22stJTFpGfhNkWxg-uz-bCw8c9g1VW_bm9=
5qHiSCPu9tgfS_Gs_OoWy-5XTMU51eIHw7tg_NNZl6gQB2Y4-zaCyJXWnJlDOWfvwcorqVUQhg0=
nOM-VOXf9A-Jh4oM" style=3D"color: #1a1a1a; text-decoration: underline;">wer=
e uncovered over the course of the 2000s</a>: some of the first examples of=
 applying ZKPs to a real-world problem.</p><p style=3D"color: #1a1a1a; font=
-size: 16px; line-height: 26px; margin: 0 0 1em 0;">Lots more was written a=
nd explored over that decade around how zero-knowledge systems could be app=
lied to identity and authentication problems. But the real inflection point=
 for zero-knowledge happened years later.</p><p style=3D"color: #1a1a1a; fo=
nt-size: 16px; line-height: 26px; margin: 0 0 1em 0;">In 2013 and 2014, zer=
o-knowledge proofs found commercial application in another system that spen=
t many years incubating in academia before being introduced to the world: c=
ryptocurrency. Despite the fact that cryptocurrencies tend to have a reputa=
tion for being good for use in illicit transactions, a <strong>core feature=
 of bitcoin and most other cryptocurrencies is that everything that happens=
 with them is publicly recorded</strong>. All you need to look at are cases=
 like the <a href=3D"https://email.mg2.substack.com/c/eJxFUc3K5CAQfJrxZvA3M=

ware crackdown</a> by the FBI to understand that bitcoin is in fact complet=

t: 26px; margin: 0 0 1em 0;">Cue the introduction of Zcash (originally call=
ed <a href=3D"https://email.mg2.substack.com/c/eJxNkM1uhDAMhJ9mc1uUXxIOOfTS=

as well as its predecessor <a href=3D"https://email.mg2.substack.com/c/eJxF=
kE2OwyAMhU9TlhG_ARYsZtNrRATcFDWBCJyZye2HNouRLNvy09OTv-ARllJPt5eG5N0mPHdwGX7=

before retracing… this stuff is volatile). All of this money and atte=
ntion has led to a dramatic acceleration of breakthroughs in zero-knowledge=
 systems, particularly when it comes to implementing them.</p><p style=3D"c=
olor: #1a1a1a; font-size: 16px; line-height: 26px; margin: 0 0 1em 0;">A ne=
w generation of efficient zero-knowledge proofs have been developed that so=
lve many of the issues that previously surrounded the clunky, burdensome, e=
xpensive, and controversial setups of these systems. (If you listened to th=
at RadioLab episode, many of the problems discussed in it are no longer pro=
blems… only 5 years later!) </p><p style=3D"color: #1a1a1a; font=
-size: 16px; line-height: 26px; margin: 0 0 1em 0;">Optimizations have led =
to huge advancements in the speed and scalability of these systems. A new a=
nd larger community of builders have cropped up leading to implementation i=
mprovements. Active open source development of libraries around these syste=
ms are accelerating development and increasing performance.</p><p style=3D"=
color: #1a1a1a; font-size: 16px; line-height: 26px; margin: 0 0 1em 0;">The=
